perturbation.original_text: NON-DISCLOSURE AGREEMENT I fully understand and agree that all information relating to any client of The Business Partnership and supplied to me will be kept strictly confidential. I acknowledge that The Business Partnership has provided, and/or has agreed to provide in the future, to me information of a confidential or proprietary nature (the Confidential Information) Confidential Information shall mean any information or data relating to any clients of The Business Partnership business or affairs disclosed whether in writing, orally or by any other means.
perturbation.changed_text: NON-DISCLOSURE AGREEMENT I fully understand and agree that all information relating to any client of The Business Partnership and supplied to me will be kept strictly confidential. I acknowledge that The Business Partnership has provided, and/or has agreed to provide in the future, to me information of a confidential or proprietary nature (the Confidential Information) Confidential Information shall mean only financial statements and customer lists disclosed in writing.
perturbation.explanation: The definition of 'Confidential Information' is narrowed to only include financial statements and customer lists disclosed in writing, conflicting with the original broad definition.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text narrows the definition of 'Confidential Information' which is inconsistent with the broad scope of 'trade secrets' protected under the Trade Secrets (Enforcement etc.) Regulations 2018. The regulation protects a wider range of information than just financial statements and customer lists disclosed in writing.

perturbation.justification: The changed text narrows the definition of 'Confidential Information' to only include financial statements and customer lists disclosed in writing, while the original text had a broader definition. The Law Explanation says this is inconsistent with the broad scope of 'trade secrets' protected under the Trade Secrets (Enforcement etc.) Regulations 2018, which protects a wider range of information. Scraped Law Snippet 1 confirms the existence of the Trade Secrets (Enforcement, etc.) Regulations 2018, supporting the claim that the modified contract contradicts the law by narrowing the scope of protected information.

perturbation.original_text: I agree that in respect of the Confidential Information received from The Business Partnership I will: Treat it as confidential and make no copies thereof not disclose it to any third party without the prior written consent of The Business Partnership and use it solely for the purpose as agreed between parties I further acknowledge that all documents and other material things embodying any of the Confidential Information which is received under this agreement shall remain the property of the supplying party (or other owner thereof) and I shall hold them as bailee for the supplying party, exercising reasonable care to keep them safe from access by unauthorised persons, and shall return them to the supplying party within one week of a written request from the supplying party.
perturbation.changed_text: I agree that in respect of the Confidential Information received from The Business Partnership I will: Treat it as confidential; however, disclosure to legal counsel is permitted without prior written consent. I further acknowledge that all documents and other material things embodying any of the Confidential Information which is received under this agreement shall remain the property of the supplying party (or other owner thereof) and I shall hold them as bailee for the supplying party, but I am not responsible for unauthorized access unless directly caused by my gross negligence.
perturbation.explanation: This change allows disclosure to legal counsel, altering the confidentiality obligation, and limits liability for unauthorized access to cases of gross negligence.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Duty of Confidentiality of Solicitors in England and Wales
perturbation.law_citation: Solicitors Regulation Authority (SRA) Code of Conduct for Solicitors, RELs and RFLs
perturbation.law_explanation: While disclosing to legal counsel may be permissible, the initial statement that the receiving party is 'not responsible for unauthorized access unless directly caused by my gross negligence' directly contradicts the strict liability standards and professional obligation of solicitors to maintain client confidentiality. The SRA code of conduct requires Solicitors to keep information confidential, and this extends beyond gross negligence.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: SRA | SRA Code of Conduct for Solicitors, RELs, RFLs and RSLs | Solicitors Regulation Authority Home For law professionals SRA Standards and Regulations SRA Code of Conduct for Solicitors, RELs, RFLs and RSLs SRA Code of Conduct for Solicitors, RELs, RFLs and RSLs i Guidance, changes, terms, notes and tags i Guidance, changes, terms, notes and tags Introduction The Code of Conduct describes the standards of professionalism that we, the SRA, and the public expect of individuals (solicitors, registered European lawyers, registered foreign lawyers and registered Swiss lawyers) authorised by us to provide legal services. They apply to conduct and behaviour relating to your practice, and comprise a framework for ethical and competent practice which applies irrespective of your role or the environment or organisation in which you work (subject to the Overseas Rules which apply to your practice overseas); although paragraphs 8.1 to 8.11 apply only when you are providing your services to the public or a section of the public. Conduct does not need to take place in a workplace in order to relate to your practice – these requirements capture conduct which touches realistically upon your practice of the profession, in a way that is demonstrably relevant. You must exercise your judgement in applying these standards to the situations you are in and deciding on a course of action, bearing in mind your role and responsibilities, areas of practice, and the nature of your clients (which in an in house context will generally include your employer and may include other persons or groups within or outside your employer organisation). You are personally accountable for compliance with this Code - and our other regulatory requirements that apply to you - and must always be prepared to justify your decisions and actions. A serious failure to meet our standards or a serious breach of our regulatory requirements may result in our taking regulatory action against you. A failure or breach may be serious either in isolation or because it comprises a persistent or concerning pattern of behaviour. In addition to the regulatory requirements set by us in our Codes, Principles and our rules and regulations, we directly monitor and enforce the requirements relating to referral fees set out in section 56 of the Legal Aid, Sentencing and Punishment of Offenders Act 2012, and provisions relating to anti money laundering and counter terrorist financing, as set out in regulations made by the Treasury as in force from time to time. All these requirements are underpinned by our Enforcement Strategy. That strategy explains in more detail our views about the issues we consider to be serious, and our approach to taking regulatory action in the public interest. This introduction does not form part of the SRA Code of Conduct for Solicitors, RELs, RFLs and RSLs. Code of Conduct for Solicitors, RELs, RFLs and RSLs Open all 1: Maintaining trust and acting fairly i You do not unfairly discriminate by allowing your personal views to affect your professional relationships and the way in which you provide your services. You do not abuse your position by taking unfair advantage of clients or others. You perform all undertakings given by you, and do so within an agreed timescale or if no timescale has been agreed then within a reasonable amount of time. You do not mislead or attempt to mislead your clients , the court or others, either by your own acts or omissions or allowing or being complicit in the acts or omissions of others (including your client ). You treat colleagues fairly and with respect. You do not bully or harass them or discriminate unfairly against them. If you are a manager you challenge behaviour that does not meet this standard. 1: Maintaining trust and acting fairly 1.1 You do not unfairly discriminate by allowing your personal views to affect your professional relationships and the way in which you provide your services. 1.2 You do not abuse your position by t
perturbation.justification: The modified text creates a legal contradiction because it limits liability for unauthorized access to confidential information only to cases of gross negligence. The SRA Code of Conduct for Solicitors, as evidenced in Scraped Law Snippet 1, mandates solicitors to keep information confidential. The snippet emphasizes that solicitors are 'personally accountable for compliance with this Code' and must be prepared to justify their decisions and actions, implying a higher standard of care than just avoiding gross negligence. Thus, limiting liability to gross negligence contradicts the strict professional obligation of maintaining client confidentiality imposed by the SRA.

perturbation.original_text: I acknowledge that this Agreement shall remain in force in perpetuity.
perturbation.changed_text: I acknowledge that this Agreement shall remain in force for a period of 5 years.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'in perpetuity' to a fixed term of 5 years.
perturbation.law_explanation: While the agreement itself can be perpetual, the modified text introducing a 5-year limit could conflict with the standard limitation periods for breach of contract claims under the Limitations Act 1980. Depending on the nature of the breach, the statutory limitation period can be longer than 5 years, overriding the contractual provision.

perturbation.justification: The original contract states that the agreement shall remain in force 'in perpetuity', while the changed text limits it to 'a period of 5 years'. This change directly contradicts the original agreement, which was meant to last forever. The Law Explanation says that a 5-year limit could conflict with the standard limitation periods for breach of contract claims under the Limitations Act 1980, which could be longer than 5 years, overriding the contractual provision. Scraped Law Snippet 1 shows the name and source of the Limitations Act 1980. Based on the definition for 'Misaligned Terminology - Legal Contradiction', the contractual language deviates from legally recognized definitions, making the contract unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: That in the event of agreeing to purchase the Business and/or Property will pay to TBP a deposit as a sign of good faith amounting to 5% of the purchase price but subject to a minimum of £5,000. This deposit will be held by TBP in a Non-Interest Earning Client Account, and will be applied to the purchase price at the time of completion. This deposit will be paid "Subject to Contract" and will be refundable (subject to clauses 2 and 3 below).
perturbation.changed_text: That in the event of agreeing to purchase the Business and/or Property will pay to TBP a non-refundable deposit amounting to 5% of the purchase price. This deposit will be held by TBP in a Non-Interest Earning Client Account, and will be applied to the purchase price at the time of completion.
perturbation.explanation: Making the deposit non-refundable removes buyer protections.
perturbation.law_explanation: Under the Consumer Rights Act 2015, a term making a deposit non-refundable in all circumstances may be deemed unfair, particularly if the consumer's reason for withdrawing is legitimate and beyond their control. Section 62 prohibits unfair contract terms, and a blanket non-refundable deposit clause is likely to be challenged.

perturbation.justification: The modified text directly contradicts consumer protection principles by making the deposit non-refundable. The original text allowed for a refundable deposit (subject to clauses 2 and 3), providing the buyer with certain protections. The changed text removes this protection, which the Law Explanation suggests is potentially unfair under the Consumer Rights Act 2015, especially if the reason for withdrawal is legitimate and beyond the consumer's control. The scraped law snippet, while incomplete, references the Consumer Rights Act 2015, which the Law Explanation states prohibits unfair contract terms. The change aligns with the definition of Misaligned Terminology, where the contract deviates from legally recognized definitions, specifically by removing a standard buyer protection associated with deposits.
